revision.comment: Text replacement - "== AUTOGENERATED CONTENT FROM WIKIDATA ==" to "== AUTOGENERATED CONTENT FROM WIKIDATA == [[Category:Pages with duplicated content]]"
revision.text:
[[ja:阿射加神社]] [[Asaka Shrine]] (阿射加神社) is a {{ill|Moto-Ise Shrines|lt=Moto-Ise Shrine|ja|元伊勢|1=Moto-Ise Shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise Shrines|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise Shrines|qq=}} {{ill|Moto-Ise Shrines|ja|元伊勢|1=Moto-Ise Shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise Shrines|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise Shrines|qq=}} are {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} that used to host Amaterasu before she was moved to [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Jingu]].{{Cite book |last=Kidder |first=J. Edward |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=7pEOicqsjv0C&dq=%22Moto-Ise%22&pg=PA201 |title=Himiko and Japan's Elusive Chiefdom of Yamatai: Archaeology, History, and Mythology |date=2007-02-28 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3035-9 |language=en|qq=}}{{Cite book |last=Stalker |first=Nancy K. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=v44BEAAAQBAJ&dq=%22Moto-Ise%22&pg=PA62 |title=Prophet Motive: Deguchi Onisaburō, Oomoto, and the Rise of New Religions in Imperial Japan |date=2007-10-31 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-6404-0 |language=en|qq=}} The first one was {{ill|Ōmiwa Shrine|lt=Hibara Shrine|ja|檜原神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmiwa Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大神神社|1=Ōmiwa Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q705542}} ({{ill|Ōmiwa Shrine|en|Ōmiwa Shrine|qid=Q705542|lt=Ōmiwa Shrine|1=Ōmiwa Shrine}}).{{Cite web |last=D |first=John |date=2011-08-10 |title=Hibara Jinja and Amaterasu |url=https://www.greenshinto.com/2011/08/11/hibara-jinja-and-amaterasu/ |access-date=2023-10-18 |website=Green Shinto |language=en-GB|qq=}}{{Cite web |title=Hibara Jinja Shrine（Nara） |url=https://www.jldb.bunka.go.jp/en/location/290100006/?search=1&q=Hibara+Shrine |access-date=2023-10-03 |website=Nationwide location database |language=en|qq=}}{{Cite web |title=Omiwa Jinja Shrine/Sai Jinja Shrine/Kuehiko Jinja Shrine/Hibara Jinja Shrine│Destinations│Discover YAMATO│YAMATO UNKNOWN ORIGIN |url=http://www.city.sakurai.lg.jp/section/yamato-travel/destination/area01/spot02/ |access-date=2023-10-03 |website=YAMATO UNKNOWN ORIGIN |language=en|qq=}} Amaterasu was originally enshrined there before eventually moving to other Moto-Ise shrines and then finally to Ise Jingu. More sources to add{{Cite book |last=Kidder |first=J. Edward |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=7pEOicqsjv0C&dq=%22Moto+Ise%22&pg=PA201 |title=Himiko and Japan's Elusive Chiefdom of Yamatai: Archaeology, History, and Mythology |date=2007-02-28 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3035-9 |language=en|qq=}}{{Cite book |last=Stalker |first=Nancy K. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=v44BEAAAQBAJ&dq=%22Moto+Ise%22&pg=PA62 |title=Prophet Motive: Deguchi Onisaburō, Oomoto, and the Rise of New Religions in Imperial Japan |date=2007-10-31 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-6404-0 |language=en|qq=}}{{Cite book |last=Authors |first=Various |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=XEIiEAAAQBAJ&dq=%22Moto+Ise%22&pg=PA534 |title=RLE: Japan Mini-Set F: Philosophy and Religion (4 vols) |date=2021-03-18 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-136-90356-4 |language=en|qq=}}{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=nPaezHFlfNQC&dq=%22Moto+Ise%22&pg=PT107 |title=Where the Trees Grow Thick |isbn=978-0-595-28240-1 |language=en|qq=}}{{Cite book |last1=Göttler |first1=Christine |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=uyk_DwAAQBAJ&dq=%22Moto+Ise%22&pg=PA437 |title=The Nomadic Object: The Challenge of World for Early Modern Religious Art |last2=Mochizuki |first2=Mia |date=2017-11-06 |publisher=BRILL |isbn=978-90-04-35450-0 |language=en|qq=}}{{Cite book |last=Picken |first=Stuart D. B. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&dq=%22Moto+Ise%22&pg=PA232 |title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto |date=2010-12-28 |publisher=Scarecrow Press |isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8 |language=en|qq=}}{{Cite book |last=Dougill |first=John |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=-H_cEAAAQBAJ&dq=%22Moto+Ise%22&pg=PT204 |title=Off the Beaten Tracks in Japan: A Journey by Train from Hokkaido to Kyushu |date=2023-11-07 |publisher=Stone Bridge Press, Inc. |isbn=978-1-61172-963-4 |language=en|qq=}}{{Cite book |last=Ponsonby-Fane |first=R. A. B. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=BY23AwAAQBAJ&dq=%22Moto+Ise%22&pg=PA555 |title=Studies In Shinto & Shrines |date=2014-06-03 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-136-89294-3 |language=en|qq=}}{{Cite book |last=Ponsonby-Fane |first=R. A. B. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=BY23AwAAQBAJ&dq=%22Moto+Ise%22&pg=PA555 |title=Studies In Shinto & Shrines |date=2014-06-03 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-136-89294-3 |language=en|qq=}}{{Cite book |last=Army |first=United States Department of the |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=PvgsAAAAIAAJ&dq=%22Moto+Ise%22&pg=RA3-PA147 |title=Pamphlet - Dept. of the Army |date=1945 |publisher=Headquarters, Department of the Army. |language=en|qq=}}{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=MVHoAAAAMAAJ&dq=%22Moto+Ise%22&pg=PA147 |title=Department of the Army Pamphlet |date=1945 |publisher=The Department |language=en|qq=}} [[ja:阿射加神社#阿射加神社（小阿坂）]] ==Overview== In the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|de|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|zh|延喜式神名帐|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}} the shrine is listed as one of the "three seats of Asaka Shrine" (阿射加神社三座), and today the {{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|qid=Q128196|lt=Mie Prefecture|1=Mie Prefecture}}'s {{ill|Matsusaka|en|Matsusaka|qid=Q460829|lt=Matsusaka|1=Matsusaka}} in the towns of Koasaka and Ōasaka host shrines of the same name regarded as its {{ill|ronsha|de|Ronsha-Schrein|ja|論社|1=candidate shrines|2=ja|3=論社|lt=candidate|zh|论社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ronsha|13=User:Immanuelle/candidate shrines|1=Engishiki_Jinmyōchō#Ronsha|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki_Jinmyocho|lt=Ronsha}}}}. Both face east on the eastern foothills of {{ill|Mount Asaka|de|Berg Asaka|ja|阿坂山|zh|阿坂山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Asaka|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, overlooking the Matsusaka Plain, share identical site conditions, and were colloquially known until the early modern era as {{ill|Ryūten Myōjin|de|Ryūten-Myōjin|ja|龍天明神|zh|龙天明神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūten Myōjin|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, indicating a shared cult; they are also less than one kilometer apart. As described below, the Shikinai Asaka Shrine is said to have originally been enshrined on Mount Asaka's summit, and theories differ on whether its relocation to the foothills occurred at Koasaka or ŌasakaKoasaka relocation: Tōdō Motomoto's Sangoku Chishi; Ōasaka relocation: Yasuoka Chikatake's Seyō Goringu Ikkyō.. Some propose that among the three seats, two correspond to these shrines and a third lies elsewhereOnmukae Kiyonao's Ise Shikinai Jinja Kenroku; Jingi Shiryō; Tokusen Shinmyōchō.. Moreover, the area was part of {{ill|Ise Shrine|en|Ise Shrine|qid=Q687168|lt=Ise Jingu|1=Ise Grand Shrine}}'s {{ill|Geku|de|Geku (Außenschrein)|ja|外宮|zh|外宫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Geku|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 豊受大神宮|1=Toyōke Daijingū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11633343}} kitchen territory ({{ill|miyado|de|Miyado|ja|御厨|zh|御厨|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/miyado|1=mikuriya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11488831}}) known as {{ill|Asaka no Miyado|de|Asaka-no-Miyado|ja|阿射賀御厨|zh|阿射贺御厨|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asaka no Miyado|1=Asaka no Miyado|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11657391}}Taijingū Shosatsujiki; Chūyūki; Azuma Kagami.. In the 1339 registry {{ill|Kyūnin Hikitsuke narabi ni Shinryō Mokuroku|de|Kyūnin Hikitsuke narabi ni Shinryō Mokuroku|ja|給人引付并神領目録|zh|给人引付并神领目录|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyūnin Hikitsuke narabi ni Shinryō Mokuroku|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, it is divided into "Ō Asaka Miyado" and "Shō Asaka Miyado," suggesting each shrine became the guardian deity of its respective territory upon partition. ===Enshrined Deities=== According to the {{ill|Kōtaijingū Gishikichō|de|Kōtaijingū Gishikichō|ja|皇太神宮儀式帳|zh|皇太神宫仪式帐|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtaijingū Gishikichō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 延暦儀式帳|1=Enryaku Gishikichō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q109596938}}, when {{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|en|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|qid=Q4388732|lt=Yamatohime no Mikoto|1=Yamatohime-no-mikoto}} enshrined {{ill|Amaterasu|en|Amaterasu|qid=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu-Ōmikami|1=Amaterasu}} at {{ill|Fujikata Katahiki-miya|de|Fujikata Katahiki-miya|ja|藤方片樋宮|zh|藤方片樋宫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujikata Katahiki-miya|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the envoy {{ill|Ahe no Ōshinehiko no Mikoto|de|Ahe no Ōshinehiko no Mikoto|ja|阿倍大稲彦命|zh|阿倍大稲彦命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ahe no Ōshinehiko no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} pacified the fierce deity {{ill|Asaka no Araburu Kami|de|Asaka-no-Araburu-Kami|ja|阿佐鹿悪神|zh|阿佐鹿恶神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asaka no Araburu Kami|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. The Wamatohime no Mikoto Seiki recounts that at "Asaka no Miko (Asaka Peak)" {{ill|Itsuhaya Furu no Kami|de|Itsuhaya-Furu-no-Kami|ja|伊豆速布留神|zh|伊豆速布留神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Itsuhaya Furu no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} obstructed passage, so a summit shrine was built and prayers offered by {{ill|Ōwakugo no Mikoto|de|Ōwakugo no Mikoto|ja|大若子命|zh|大若子命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōwakugo no Mikoto|1=Ōwakako no Mikoto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q111114032}}, ancestor of the Geku priests {{ill|Watarai|de|Watarai|ja|度会氏|zh|度会氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Watarai|1=Watarai clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11486123}}. Another source describes an {{ill|Kōjin|en|Kōjin|qid=Q6455190|lt=Araruru Kami|1=Kōjin}} on "Asakayama" blocking Yamatohime's pilgrimage to the "Isuzugawa no Miya" (present-day {{ill|Naikū|de|Naikū|ja|内宮|zh|内宫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naikū|1=Naikū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q23996212}}) of {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|lt=Kōtai Jingū|lt=Kōtaijingū|de|Kōtai Jingū|lt=Kōtai Jingū|lt=Kōtaijingū|ja|皇大神宮|zh|皇大神宫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtaijingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}. To pacify this "wild deity," {{ill|Amenohiwake no Mikoto|de|Amenohiwake-no-Mikoto|ja|天日別命|zh|天日别命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amenohiwake no Mikoto|1=Amihiwake no Mikoto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q106107046}}'s descendant Ōwakugo no Mikoto enshrined it and built the shrine…. These deities, originally rulers of the region, later merged with rice-field water cults, earning the popular name {{ill|Ryūten Myōjin|de|Ryūten-Myōjin|ja|龍天明神|zh|龙天明神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūten Myōjin|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Regarding the three-seat theory, {{ill|Deguchi Nobutsune|de|Deguchi Nobutsune|ja|出口延経|zh|出口延经|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Deguchi Nobutsune|1=Deguchi Nobutsune|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q108780926}} proposed that the shrine honors the three spirits of {{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|lt=Sarutahiko Ōkami|lt=Sarutahiko|de|Sarutahiko Ōkami|lt=Sarutahiko Ōkami|lt=Sarutahiko|ja|猿田彦神|zh|猿田彦神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to サルタヒコ}} who drowned in Asaka, called Sokodoku no Mitama, Tsubutatsu no Mitama, and Awasaku no MitamaShinmyōchō Kōshō. {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|en|Motoori Norinaga|qid=Q384647|lt=Motoori Norinaga|1=Motoori Norinaga}} adopted this view, which aligns the "wild deity" motif with Sarutahiko's role in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|qid=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}}. Both Asaka Shrines today enshrine Sarutahiko and Itsuhaya Furu no Kami. Ancient {{ill|Ama|en|Ama|qid=Q228873|lt=Ama|1=Ama}} regarded the number three as sacred, as seen with {{ill|Watatsumi Sanjin|de|Watatsumi Sanjin|ja|ワタツミ三神|zh|海神三神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Watatsumi Sanjin|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135503366|1=Watatsumi}}, {{ill|Sumiyoshi sanjin|en|Sumiyoshi sanjin|qid=Q1114013|lt=Sumiyoshi Sanjin|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin}}, and {{ill|Three Munakata Goddesses|lt=Three Munakata Goddesses|lt=Munakata Sanjoshin|de|Three Munakata Goddesses|lt=Three Munakata Goddesses|lt=Munakata Sanjoshin|ja|宗像三女神|zh|宗像三女神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata Sanjoshin|qq=}}, suggesting the original Ama veneration of Sarutahiko's three spirits gave rise to these traditions…. ===Divine Ranks=== In {{ill|Shōwa 2 (835)|de|Shōwa-Zeit 2 (835)|ja|承和2年|zh|承和2年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōwa 2 (835)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135503367|1=835}} on {{ill|December 14|en|December 14|qid=Q2382|lt=December 14|1=December 14}}, the shrine was granted Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade (従五位下) in the {{ill|Shoku Nihon Kōki|en|Shoku Nihon Kōki|qid=Q1041301|lt=Shoku Nihon Kōki|1=Shoku Nihon Kōki}}. In {{ill|Kajō 3 (850)|de|Kajō-Zeit 3 (850)|ja|嘉祥3年|zh|嘉祥3年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kajō 3 (850)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135503370|1=850}} on {{ill|October 7|en|October 7|qid=Q2926|lt=October 7|1=October 7}}, it advanced to Junior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade (従五位上); in {{ill|Saikō 2 (855)|de|Saikō-Zeit 2 (855)|ja|斉衡2年|zh|斉衡2年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saikō 2 (855)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135503372|1=855}} on {{ill|January 21|en|January 21|qid=Q2273|lt=January 21|1=January 21}} it was designated a Myōjin Shrine (名神預), and on {{ill|January 25|en|January 25|qid=Q2279|lt=January 25|1=January 25}} promoted to Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade (従四位下)…. Further promotions in {{ill|Jōgan 1 (859)|de|Jōgan-Zeit 1 (859)|ja|貞観元年|zh|贞观元年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōgan 1 (859)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135503374|1=859}} on {{ill|January 27|en|January 27|qid=Q2284|lt=January 27|1=January 27}} and in {{ill|Jōgan 8 (866)|de|Jōgan-Zeit 8 (866)|ja|貞観8年|zh|贞观8年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōgan 8 (866)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135503377|1=866}} on {{ill|November 4|en|November 4|qid=Q2994|lt=November 4|1=November 4}} raised it to Third Rank (従三位), as recorded in the {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|en|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|qid=Q1052981|lt=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|1=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku}}. Under the Engishiki, all three seats are listed as Myōjin Taisha, reflecting the court’s esteem. The {{ill|Ise Province Shrine Register|de|Ise-Kun Kokunai Shinmyōchō|ja|伊勢国神名帳|zh|伊势国神名帐|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Province Shrine Register|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} records it as "Asaka Daijin" in second place among eleven shrines. == Azaka Shrine == [[ja:阿射加神社]] [[de:Asaka-Schrein]] {{Infobox religious building | name = '''Azaka Shrine''' (阿射加神社) | image = File:Azaka-jinja (Koazakacho, Matsusaka, Mie), honden-2.jpg|280px | caption = Main hall | location = {{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|qid=Q128196|lt=Mie Prefecture|1=Mie Prefecture}}, {{ill|Matsusaka|en|Matsusaka|qid=Q460829|lt=Matsusaka City|1=Matsusaka}} Koazakacho 120 | coordinates = {{Coord|34.590861|N|136.469194|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = '''Sarutahiko-no-Ōkami'''
'''Izuhatatsuhime-no-Kami'''
'''Ryūten-no-Ōkami''' | shrine_status = {{ill|Shikinai Shrine|de|Shikinai-Schrein|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|zh|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinai Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|de|Myōjin-Taisha|ja|名神大社|zh|名神大社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} 3 seats) {{ill|Ronsya|de|Ronsya|ja|論社|1=candidate shrines|2=ja|3=論社|lt=candidate|zh|论社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ronsya|13=User:Immanuelle/candidate shrines|1=Engishiki_Jinmyōchō#Ronsha|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki_Jinmyocho|lt=Ronsha}}| prefecture= {{ill|Matsusaka|qid=Q460829|1=Matsusaka}} }}
Former {{ill|Village Shrine|de|Dorfschrein|ja|村社|1=Son-sha|qid=Q134917284}}{{efn|Former shrine rank|lt=Son-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|zh|村社|1=Son-sha|qid=Q134917284}}{{efn|Former shrine rank|lt=Son-sha|1=Modern_system_of_ranked_Shinto_shrines#Village_shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Village Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q712534}} | established = (legend) {{ill|Eiroku|en|Eiroku|qid=Q1143341|lt=Eiroku era|1=Eiroku}} (1558–1570) | architecture = {{ill|Kirizuma-zukuri|de|Kirizuma-zukuri|ja|切妻造|zh|切妻造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kirizuma-zukuri|1=Kirizuma-style|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q13308575}}, gabled entrance | annual_festival = Sunday nearest to {{ill|October 8|en|October 8|qid=Q2924|lt=October 8|1=October 8}} | rituals = {{ill|Hibi-dameshi|de|Hibi-Dameshi|ja|御火試|zh|御火试|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hibi-dameshi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Kayu-dameshi|de|Kayu-Dameshi|ja|粥試神事|zh|粥试神事|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kayu-dameshi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Kanko dance|de|Kanko-Tanz|ja|羯鼓踊り|zh|羯鼓舞|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanko dance|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (January 14) at subsidiary {{ill|Ōwakako Shrine|de|Ōwakako-Schrein|ja|大若子神社|zh|大若子神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōwakako Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} | map = {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|35|27.1|N|136|28|09.1|E}}|title=Azaka Shrine (Koazakacho, Matsusaka)}} }} ===Enshrined Deities=== The current enshrined deities are: * '''Sarutahiko-no-Ōkami''' * '''Izuhatatsuhime-no-Kami''' * '''Ryūten-no-Ōkami''' In {{ill|Miko Kiyonobu|de|Miko Kiyonobu|ja|御巫清直|zh|御巫清直|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miko Kiyonobu|1=Mikannagi Kiyonao|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q6537624}}'s ''{{ill|Ise Shikinai Jinja Kenroku|de|Ise Shikinai-Schrein-Kenroku|ja|伊勢式内神社撿録|zh|伊势式内神社抄录|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Shikinai Jinja Kenroku|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'', it is recorded: “Three halls exist. … The central one is called Ryūten Daimyōjin. … To the north is the Shimmei Shrine; to the south is the Kumano Shrine,” describing the conditions from the late {{ill|Bakumatsu|en|Bakumatsu|qid=Q804558|lt=Bakumatsu|1=Bakumatsu}} through early {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji|1=Meiji era}}. ===History=== According to shrine tradition, the shrine was relocated from the summit of Mount Asaka during the {{ill|Eiroku|en|Eiroku|qid=Q1143341|lt=Eiroku era|1=Eiroku}} (1558–1570) to avoid destruction by warfare. However, in {{ill|Mikuni Chishi|de|Mikuni Chishi|ja|三国地志|zh|三国地志|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikuni Chishi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Tōdō Motohogo|de|Tōdō Motohogo|ja|藤堂元甫|zh|藤堂元甫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōdō Motohogo|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} states that during the {{ill|Ōnin War|en|Ōnin War|qid=Q385676|lt=Ōnin War|1=Ōnin War}} (1467–1477) the {{ill|Kitabatake clan|de|Kitabatake-Klan|ja|北畠氏|zh|北畠氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitabatake clan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 北畠家}} moved it to its present site. Moreover, ''{{ill|Kojiki-den|en|Kojiki-den|qid=Q11410835|lt=Kojiki-den|1=Kojiki-den}}'' mentions that according to the temple Enza Yakushi in Koazaka, {{ill|Gyōki|en|Gyōki|qid=Q1136664|lt=Gyōki|1=Gyōki}} enshrined this kami. On February 20, 1908, the shrine was granted village shrine status, and after World War II it became affiliated with the Association of Shinto Shrines. ===Rituals=== * '''Hibi-dameshi''', '''Kayu-dameshi''' (January 14) at subsidiary {{ill|Ōwakako Shrine|de|Ōwakako-Schrein|ja|大若子神社|zh|大若子神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōwakako Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}: The Hibi-dameshi ritual involves pressing the tip of oak burned in an irori hearth against panels marked with months to predict weather from the ash color. The Kayu-dameshi ritual uses a bamboo tube in red-bean porridge; the amounts of rice and beans inside the tube are used for harvest divination. These were designated a city intangible folk cultural property in 1965. * '''Kanko dance''' (January 14): Originally a rainmaking dance in early summer, later moved to autumn after plentiful harvest years. Now performed by children around a great bonfire (''Sagyōchō''), beginning with ''seko-iri'' chants, expressing gratitude to the kami and praying for a good harvest. The ensemble consists of large and small kanko drums, a conch shell trumpet, and a red-streamer pole, led by the streamer bearer. Designated a prefectural intangible folk cultural property in 1973. ===Priests=== The Sugawara and Kajiwara families traditionally served as priests, but the Kajiwara line died out during the {{ill|Kyōhō|en|Kyōhō|qid=Q1145048|lt=Kyōhō era|1=Kyōhō}} (1716–1736), and the Sugawara family ceased hereditary succession in the Meiji period. There was also a head temple, Saizōji on Mount Kodera, abolished in 1872 due to lack of clergy. It is said that {{ill|Tōmyōzan Kōtokuji|de|Tōmyōzan Kōtokuji|ja|景徳寺 (松阪市)|zh|景德寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōmyōzan Kōtokuji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (East Bright Mountain Kōtokuji), about 600 m west, served as the Oku-no-in. ===Shrine Buildings=== The main shrine consists of three single-bay {{ill|Kirizuma-zukuri|de|Kirizuma-zukuri|ja|切妻造|zh|切妻造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kirizuma-zukuri|1=Kirizuma-style|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q13308575}} gabled structures, each with extended ''chigi'' and ''katsuogi''. There is also a thatched ''shōnoya'' building for the Hibi-dameshi and Kayu-dameshi rituals. ===Subsidiary Shrines=== '''Ōwakako Shrine''': Main deity is Ōwakako no Mikoto. In 1920, the subsidiary shrines of Ame-no-Hiwake Shrine (formerly Hachiman Shrine, which had merged five shrines including Kifune Shrine) and Yakumo Shrine (which in 1912 had merged nine local unranked shrines such as Atago, Tsushima, Akiba, and mountain shrines) were combined, enshrining 12 deities: Sumiyoshi Ōkami, Ukanomitama no Kami, Ame-no-Hiwake no Mikoto, Kanayamahiko no Kami, Ōyamatsumi no Kami, Kagutsuchi no Kami, Emperor Ōjin (Homuda…), Kifune Ōkami, Sugawara Michizane, Hachioji Ōkami, Susanoo no Mikoto, and one unspecified deity. ===Cultural Properties=== ====Mie Prefecture Designated Cultural Property==== * Intangible Folk Cultural Property: Koazakacho Kanko Dance ====Matsusaka City Designated Cultural Property==== * Natural Monument: Forest of Azaka Shrine – An almost pristine evergreen broadleaf forest dominated by Castanopsis sieboldii about 15 m tall and 1 m diameter at breast height, with species such as Machilus thunbergii, Symplocos lucida, Saraca japonica, and others. Designated in 1988. * Intangible Folk Cultural Property: Hibi-dameshi and Kayu-dameshi rituals File:Azaka-jinja (Koazakacho, Matsusaka, Mie), shasou.jpg|Forest of Azaka Shrine (Matsusaka City Natural Monument) File:Azaka-jinja (Koazakacho, Matsusaka, Mie), keidaisha.jpg|Subsidiary Ōwakako Shrine File:Azaka-jinja (Koazakacho, Matsusaka, Mie), torii.jpg|Torii gate '''Azaka Shrine''' (阿射加神社 (松阪市小阿坂町)) [[ja:阿射加神社 (松阪市小阿坂町)]][[de:Azaka-Schrein (Koazakacho, Matsusaka)]] ==Another Azaka Shrine== {{Infobox religious building | name = Azaka Shrine | native_name = 阿射加神社 | native_name_lang = ja | image = Azaka-jinja (Oazakacho, Matsusaka, Mie), honden-2.jpg | image_size = 280px | caption = Main hall | location = Ōazakachō 670, Matsusaka, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|qid=Q128196|lt=Mie Prefecture|1=Mie Prefecture}}, Japan | coordinates = {{coord|34|35|53.90|N|136|28|6.23|E|display=inline,title}} | deity = '''Sarutahiko Ōkami''' (猿田彦大神); '''Izuhayafuru no Kami''' (伊豆速布留神); '''Sukadachiku no Kami''' (底度久神) | shrine_class = {{ill|Shikinaisha|de|Shikinai-Sha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|zh|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|de|Myojin-Taisha|ja|名神大社|zh|名神大社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Myōjin Taisha|qid=Q9610964}}, 3 seats) {{ill|disputed shrine|de|umstrittener Schrein|ja|論社|1=candidate shrines|2=ja|3=論社|lt=candidate|zh|论社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/disputed shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/candidate shrines|1=Engishiki_Jinmyōchō#Ronsha|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki_Jinmyocho|lt=Ronsha}}}}; formerly {{ill|village shrine|de|Dorf-Schrein|ja|村社|1=Son-sha|qid=Q134917284}}{{efn|Former shrine rank|lt=Son-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|zh|村社|1=Son-sha|qid=Q134917284}}{{efn|Former shrine rank|lt=Son-sha|1=Modern_system_of_ranked_Shinto_shrines#Village_shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/village shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q712534}} | established = Unknown | architectural_style= {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|en|Shinmei-zukuri|qid=Q930545|lt=Shinmei-zukuri|1=Shinmei-zukuri}} (cypress-bark roof) | festival = October 10 {{citation needed}} | map_type = Japan Mie Prefecture | map_frame = yes | map_zoom = 13 }} ===Rituals=== Until around Shōwa 8 (1933), a folk dance called kanko-odori was performed every year on the festival day of October 8, but it has since fallen into disuse, as recorded in the Matsusaka City history. According to shrine tradition, October 8 was known as the "Amazake Festival" (white sake festival). The kanko-odori of Ōazakachō lacked the "Sekoiriori dance" found in the kanko-odori of neighboring Koazaka. Songbooks indicate that the dance was already practiced before Bunsei 9 (1812). ===Enshrined Deities=== The current enshrined deities (kami) are the following three: * '''Sarutahiko Ōkami''' * '''Izuhayafuru no Kami''' * '''Sukadachiku no Kami''' In the music record ''Ise Shikinaijinja Kenroku'', it is noted: : "At the main hall, with three doors (…excerpt…), the names of the three kami of Awasaku no Mitama are inscribed (…excerpt…). Locals refer to only one seat as Ryūten Daimyōjin. The small shrine to the north is called Shinmei, and the small shrine to the south is called Kumano Shrine, which has been the sole practice in Koazaka Village." ===History=== In ''Seiyō Goringo Yikō'' by {{ill|Yasuoka Oyataka|de|Yasuoka Oyataka|ja|安岡親毅|zh|安冈亲毅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yasuoka Oyataka|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, it is recorded that after the {{ill|Ōei|en|Ōei|qid=Q1193078|lt=Ōei era|1=Ōei}}, the shrine was relocated from the summit of {{ill|Mount Asaka|de|Berg Asaka|ja|阿坂山|zh|阿坂山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Asaka|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Shrine tradition holds that when {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|qid=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga|1=Oda Nobunaga}} attacked {{ill|Ōmiya Nyūdō Gannin-sai|de|Ōmiya Nyūdō Gannin-sai|ja|大宮入道含忍斎|zh|大宮入道含忍斎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmiya Nyūdō Gannin-sai|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the chief retainer of the {{ill|Kitabatake clan|en|Kitabatake clan|qid=Q11403853|lt=Kitabatake clan|1=Kitabatake clan}} defending {{ill|Azaka Castle|en|Azaka Castle|qid=Q5640634|lt=Asaka Castle|1=Azaka Castle}} at the mountain summit, the shrine buildings and all ancient documents were destroyed by fire. Although rebuilt after the fall of the Kitabatake clan, the shrine remained unmanaged until Genroku 3 (1690), when a shrine attendant named {{ill|Okumura Yōnin|de|Okumura Yōnin|ja|奥村要人|zh|奥村要人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okumura Yōnin|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} emerged. On March 20, 1908 (Meiji 41), the shrine was ranked as a village shrine, and after World War II it came under the jurisdiction of the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|en|Association of Shinto Shrines|qid=Q908077|lt=Association of Shinto Shrines|1=Association of Shinto Shrines}}. ===Shrine Building=== The main hall (honden) is a {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|en|Shinmei-zukuri|qid=Q930545|lt=Shinmei-zukuri|1=Shinmei-zukuri}} structure, three bays wide and two bays deep, with a cypress-bark roof. ===Subsidiary Shrines=== Eight smaller shrines are located within the precincts: * '''Dainichi Shrine''' (''Ōhirume-no-Mikoto'', a local name for {{ill|Amaterasu|en|Amaterasu|qid=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|1=Amaterasu}}) * '''Ukahiko Shrine''' (''Kishihime no Mikoto''; ''Yoshihiko no Mikoto'') * '''Hayatamao Shrine''' (''Kotosakanomikoto'') * '''Amenohibino Shrine''' (''Ame-no-Hibino no Mikoto'') * '''Tatemikarishi Shrine''' (''Tateshikonomikoto'') * '''Ōkuninushi Shrine''' (''Ōkuninushi no Mikoto'') * '''Okutsuhiko Shrine''' (''Okutsuhiko no Mikoto''; ''Okutsuhime no Mikoto'') * '''Ōwakako Shrine''' (''Ōwakako no Mikoto''; ''Susanoo no Mikoto''; ''Hondawake no Mikoto''; ''Izanami no Mikoto''; ''Ame-no-Koyane no Mikoto''; ''Ōmiya-hime no Mikoto''; ''Hachioji Ōkami'') According to the chronicle ''Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki'', the two deities enshrined at Ukahiko Shrine are the children of Ukano-hiko, ancestor of the {{ill|Taki clan|de|Clan Taki|ja|多気連|zh|多气连|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taki clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, who dedicated rice and hemp fields to {{ill|Amaterasu|en|Amaterasu|qid=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|1=Amaterasu}}; likewise, the deity of Tatemikarishi Shrine is the ancestor of the Ichishi Kuni no Miyatsuko, who similarly offered hemp fields and rice paddies to the Kamobe. Moreover, Ōwakako Shrine was established in Taishō 1 (1912) through the merger of nearby unranked shrines: Ōwakako Shrine, Hachiman Shrine, Yakumo Shrine, and Yahashira Shrine. File:Azaka-jinja (Oazakacho, Matsusaka, Mie), haiden.jpg|Haiden (Worship hall) File:Azaka-jinja (Oazakacho, Matsusaka, Mie), keidaisha.jpg|Subsidiary shrines within the precincts File:Azaka-jinja (Oazakacho, Matsusaka, Mie), torii.jpg|Torii gate [[ja:阿射加神社 (松阪市大阿坂町)]] [[de:Azaka-Schrein (Ōazakachō, Matsusaka)]] ==List of Motoise Shrines== {| class="wikitable" style="background:#ffffff; white-space:nowrap; font-size:85%" |- ! A. B.|date=2014-06-03|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-136-89294-3|language=en|qq=}} |- ! rowspan="3" |{{ill|Tanba Province|en|Tanba Province|qid=Q954511|lt=Tanba Province|1=Tanba Province}} |{{ill|8=every|Kono Shrine#Kago Shrine|en|Kono Shrine#Kago Shrine|lt=Kago Shrine|lt2=籠神社|simple|User:Immanuelle/Kono Shrine#Kago Shrine|10=ja|11=籠神社#奥宮（Keigai-Sessha）|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/8=every|qq=}} |- |{{ill|8=every|Kōtai Shrine|lt=Kōtai Shrine|lt=Kotai Shrine|ja|皇大神社 (福知山市)|lt=Kōtai Shrine|lt=Kotai Shrine|lt2=皇大神社|en:draft|simple|User:Immanuelle/Kotai Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/8=every|qq=}} |- |{{ill|8=every|Takeno Shrine|ja|竹野神社|en:draft|simple|User:Immanuelle/Takeno Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/8=every|qq=}} |- !
